summary: Gruber responds to Eric Jackson's Forbes piece criticizing Apple's capital return program and arguing Apple should pursue more M&A. While partly agreeing that Apple missed opportunities on companies like Netflix and Instagram, Gruber argues the timing critique is off — those acquisitions needed to happen earlier than Jackson suggests. He defends Apple's approach of organic innovation over acquisition-driven growth, arguing their massive cash hoard is itself a strategic weapon. Gruber is skeptical that dividends have actually helped Apple's stock price and prefers the old strategy of simply letting cash accumulate. His core position: Apple should invent its future, not buy it.
key_points: Apple's missed acquisition opportunities (Netflix, Instagram, Facebook) were timing problems — by the time critics suggested buying them, the price had already ballooned
Apple's dividend program hasn't demonstrably rewarded the stock price compared to the hypothetical of continued cash hoarding
Apple's enormous cash reserve functions as a strategic weapon whose power grows the larger it gets
An Apple-owned Instagram would likely have remained iOS-only, fundamentally changing its trajectory
Apple's identity as a company is rooted in organic innovation, not acquisition-driven growth
money_quotes: Apple needs to do what it has always done. They need to invent their future, not buy it.
Apple's cash is its Death Star. The bigger it grows, the better.
Dividends are something big traditional corporations do. Apple certainly is big, but they've never been traditional, and never should be.
I don't think Apple's dividends are holding back their M&A in any way. There is no company they should buy that they can't because they don't have enough money.
An argument about whether Apple should have bought Netflix needs to have started in 2012, not 2014.
Their success is entirely rooted on the company having a different viewpoint.
I don't think Apple has been rewarded for this capital return policy.
key_insight: Apple's massive cash hoard is more valuable as an ever-growing strategic weapon than as dividends or acquisition currency, because Apple's identity depends on inventing its future rather than buying it.
